text: 17-year-old Leen Hijaz says remarks she made at her high school graduation were cut off after she mentioned people suffering in different countries around the world. Her school district says administrators intervened so the "school-sponsored event remained consistent with its intended purpose."

Brava to this young lady for speaking up because that was exactly her intention to shed light on the very real issues happening around the world. When I see adults respond by trying to silence or censor young people, it genuinely upsets me because that makes you part of the problem, not the solution. Young people deserve the right to express themselves and be heard.

Growth requires us to face hard things. A diamond requires pressure beyond our comprehension to form. We cannot grow or evolve as a society if we are too sensitive to listen to what people are actually experiencing. Growth is often uncomfortable. Sometimes it even feels like a death of old ideas and beliefs. But when we do the hard work, the result is healing, understanding, and progress.

I know this young lady will take this moment, this love, and this support and allow it to further fuel her passion for advocacy and positive change. May she continue to use her voice boldly and fearlessly in service of humanity.

Her whole purpose should stay hidden, like her words, and stuttering… More people have been kicked out of the country in all democrat governments… Yes, I know, it hurts… Google it…. And btw, Im an immigrant, and I completely agree with you being able to kick out people from your house when u didnt invite them… why other countries are not doing something… she mixing Iran, Palestine, Congo or what with ICE, ninja please you shouldn’t graduate; honestly apparently that school is terrible so people like you come out regularly

@cnn

Her message falls apart before it even begins. The hesitation, the contradictions, the confusion, it was hard to tell whether she was talking about immigration, foreign policy, war, refugees, or simply repeating whatever slogan was popular this week.

People should probably spend more time understanding the issues they’re speaking about before turning a graduation stage into a political rally.

And for the record, I’m an immigrant. I came here legally, built a life here, and I fully understand the basic concept that every country has the right to enforce its laws and control its borders. That’s not radical. That’s common sense.

What I find fascinating is how quickly some people jump between completely different conflicts, countries, and issues as if they’re all the same conversation. One minute it’s immigration. The next it’s foreign wars. Then it’s something happening on another continent. Then somehow it all circles back to the same political talking points.

At some point, facts matter.

Context matters.

Reality matters.

And if you’re going to stand in front of a graduating class and present yourself as informed, you should probably understand the difference between passion and knowledge.

The tide is turning, and everyone can see it.

For years, the loudest voices acted like they owned the conversation. They believed they represented the majority. They don’t. More and more people are tired of the lectures, the hypocrisy, the double standards, the censorship, the outrage addiction, and the endless attempts to shame anyone who refuses to repeat the approved talking points.

The real world is not built by activists on social media. It’s built by people who work, create, invest, innovate, take risks, build businesses, raise families, solve problems, and take responsibility for their own lives.

I’m tired of being lectured by people who produce very little but somehow believe they should dictate how everyone else should live.

The future belongs to builders, not complainers. To creators, not professional critics. To people who solve problems, not people who profit from outrage.
